speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6. March 9, 1997. Survivor is Al Roland, being interviewed by Toni Katsh Binstock in Denver, Colorado, United States of America. And the interview is being done in English. At the end of the last tape, you were saying you didn't stay in the barbers business too long. So finish about what you did then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, being in the barber business, I had a chance to meet all kinds of people, businesspeople and all kinds of people. And I always try to learn from other people. And people were always happy to tell about their successes and failures.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was listening carefully and talking. So I got to know a guy that came from Israel not too long ago. He worked at the Beth Israel Hospital on Lowell and Meade. So he was also talking about doing something on his own. And he was sharp enough to figure out that the old age home business, like nursing homes, would be something worthwhile to look into and if it's possible to materialize. So he found out. He worked in the kitchen, where most of the expenses are in the kitchen. He said to feed a patient cost only $1.80 a day or something. And the nursing homes get paid for the patient I think about $6 or $7 a day. So he figured out the nurses a little bit here and there, and there's something left for the owner too. So he was inquiring and I was inquiring, and it looks promising. But how they get into?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, before we were thinking about this, we bought a building, him and I, on East 16 and Washington. An old building. But we pieced a little together, borrowed here, borrowed there for a down payment. We took a second mortgage. We got into it. So we already got something. There wasn't much of an income, but you had something solid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family here? I forgot to ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you bring them here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife came to Denver after I was here six months. And we moved into an apartment on Vrain and then on Wolf Street. And I worked as a barber, so I accumulated. I was a good saver. We needed very little to live, because we didn't live like Americans lived. We would like in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I accumulated a little money. And I wanted to own my own, the American dream. It looked promising. It looked sweet. So I lived on Wolf Street. I moved to 13th and Wolf, in a double. So I figured if I buy the double, I can rent out the basement. I can rent out, in the one apartment, three rentals. So I can pay the mortgage from it. And maybe it's going to be free rent for me and I got something. So good enough, I bought a double adjacent to where I lived. I overpaid, but I wanted to buy it, so I didn't care how much. Just to own something. So we bought it and lived over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we bought that building with a partner. So it was a lot of problems, because it was an old place and the previous owner didn't take care of the place. They were just putting Band-Aids. If there was a leak, they would tape around. So almost every day there were leaks here and leaks there. So if we had a leak, I told my partner we're going to put new copper wire, a length of copper wire so one problem would be done. That's what we did. Every time there was a leak, throw out the old galvanized. It was rusted. And we're going to get rid of the problems. And that's what we did. The middle of the night they're calling us. The weekend. We didn't have no peace. So that's what I did. Little by little we were able to care of take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here, since we decided to get a nursing home, so we figured we going to take the building and swap it for a nursing home. We got already an equity. We paid for the building about $55,000. We could take already after a year it went up, we could take 85. So with $30,000 we can do something. So we found a real estate guy and he found a nursing home that was the biggest in Denver at that time, 80-- that was before they built new nursing homes. They were old, those conversions on Capitol Hill. It was just a starting industry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had this nursing home was a dormitory from a religious seminary on Simms Street, 16th and Simms. And the owner, the major stockholder was already himself in a nursing home in a wheelchair. And when he saw us, that we are willing to go in and make something out of it, so he was encouraged to sell it to us. One owner, the minority owner, was the administrator. That nursing home had 85 beds, 35 empties, and we don't know nothing about nursing homes and we wanted to take it. They wanted $275,000. I said, I give him $200,000. My partner said, you're crazy. You're going to kill the deal. I said, to give more I got always time. The real estate, he wanted to kill me. I'm not going to take an offer like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, listen, you're real estate, take it, and it's up to them to decide, not for you. What do you think? Next day, he comes back and they decided to take it, because those two major stockholders, they had enough. They were rich people. So you went into the nursing home business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And what do you think? I wrote down the offer to make the closing in three months. I figured maybe in three months there will be a miracle, and it's going to fill up. And what you think happened? I don't know whether you remember or not. In Pueblo they had a lot of patients, retarded people, and they  were already over 65. Over there, the government had to pay $30 a day. In a nursing home they had to pay $7.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they decided they're going to shift them into nursing homes and save so much money. And they did us the best, the biggest favor. Not only our nursing home. We filled it up. All the nursing homes in the area got filled up, because there was a lot of patients there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got even eight patients more than we were licensed, but we had the house where the administrator lived. Upstairs they had four bedrooms. We put beds in. We put them over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the nursing home business then?

speaker: interviewee
text: With this partner, it wasn't too long. He was not the best guy to- you know [INAUDIBLE]? He was not the best guy to be in business with. So with him, I was I don't know, a year or two probably. But then I got together a group with Dr. [? Penslow ?] and her brother. I brought them from Chicago over here and put them in business. And Henry [? Seligman ?] got in with the us. And we bought a nursing home on Eaton, in Lakewood, was 180 beds. And I was the administrator.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school to become an administrator?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went to classes. But then, they came out with compulsory. You had to be certified, and that was a hard examination. I never had such a hard exam like they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you took that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took it, sure. I was not afraid. I learned and I made it, but I came out-- there was two days-- my head was like that big swollen up after all those questions. It was really hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you passed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I passed, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long-- are you still active in that business or  are you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the government gives a hard time. I don't know now how it is, but they made inspections every six months. And they wrote down things that should be improved. First of all, that building was a new building, and the building plans were approved by the health department. And they still found things that are not according to what they want it to be. Every six months they brought new things. This has to be done and this. You might think they go through the building and everything is OK, so it should be OK. No. Every six months, like a punch in the stomach, again and again. I got sick and tired of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: : So when did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '71 or '72. I don't remember. So we sold it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do in your retirement now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was investing a little bit and I was still taking care, I had a building. I was taking care of it. My health is not the best, so I was just able to take care of what I have. Until recently, I sold the building. Times were very bad. We had 8 or 10 years in rentals. The worst tenant I had on South Federal and Colorado Avenue. The worst. Drug dealers and drunks. They didn't pay the rent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody would buy it. I didn't know what to do with it. I had to hang in there and pay the bills to survive. It was not easy. But now things are better, my wife said, it's better now, let's hold onto it. I said, at my age what do you expect?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to have a little free time to enjoy for myself. And I sold it to other people. They were oriental people. I think they're doing all right. I don't even go there. I don't want to look at it anymore. I had enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what are your hobbies and interests now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I like to go to the Center and work out, swim a little. And then I'm with friends. I used to play chess, but I found it too aggravating. My nerves are not good. I'm thinking to go back at JCC. They have groups that play chess. I'm not a card player. I'm not a gambler, so I don't like cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you some other kind of questions kind of in closing here. How do you think that your experiences in the war affected the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I came to America, I saw that people are mistreated, like black people. In Chicago, I happened to live in a Jewish neighborhood which black people took already a hold in that neighborhood, and people were running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they were selling their houses for half the money or whatever. They're running. I said, what are the people running? What is there to run? They are people like anybody else. They are God's children. What is there to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we went through this thing. There's nothing wrong with them. They want to work. They want to live just like anybody else. So I never taught my kids about hatred. As a matter of fact, my Bernie, he was about six years old or seven years old. He was playing in the backyard on 16th Street in Chicago. Now it's all black. And he was playing with at the next-door building with a black kid, and that kid, I don't know what happened, he hit him. He came home, he cries.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what are you crying? He said that dirty boy hit me. He didn't know that he's born black. He thought he is dirty. I didn't teach my kids about that. As a matter of fact, he got married to a Gentile girl. She's wonderful, a wonderful person.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids picked to be Jewish. We had bat mitzvahs, both girls-- that's Hillary and Michelle. And she wouldn't mind to convert, my sister-in-law, but my son is not so-- he had a bad experience of [INAUDIBLE] with the rabbis, so I didn't want to-- it's not important. So he doesn't care to be converted. He lives in Evergreen, and Evergreen is not the easiest thing. There's no shul or nothing. But the kids learned, they had beautiful bat mitzvahs, they said the Torah. Very nice and beautiful. I have tapes from the bat mitzvah. So it's nice. The kids are very, very nicely behaved. They are of good background, come from a good home. We just had our 50th anniversary yesterday, the 8th of March. So we had the family together, the cousins. And my son, my older son said, thanks to my parents we are a close-knit family. And this is the main thing. Children have to feel that they have a good family, they have a good home. They have good support from parents. This is what they learn, and this is important for children. If children don't feel that they have support from mom and dad, they get raised by the street with drugs and what have you. All the bad things. If they know they can come home and discuss hardships or good times with their parents without being afraid, that's the main thing. This is part of bringing up good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any long term effects of the Holocaust? You said physically you have some things. Any emotional scars, or any other physical things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, emotional, yes. Physical too. But one thing I might say is that I myself was raised in a home where we didn't know no hatred. We didn't learn to hate people. As a matter of fact, after the war, after we came back from Russia, we were, like I told you, [? Selesia ?] in [? Liegnitz. ?] We were in the station, at the railroad station. There was a woman with a child on her arm and a child by her hand. They didn't have food. She was begging. I took five marks-- at that time they were marks-- and I gave them. I don't feel bad at all the Germans. Whoever were the murderers, they should be punished. But the people are not-- Like some Gentile people said, all the Jews are bad. All the Jews are Christ-killers. That's what in Poland they said. All the Jews are Christ-killers. This is wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if the Jews killed Christ. I think the Romans killed Christ, not the Jews. But the Romans, the Vatican is in Rome, so they can't say that the Romans killed Christ. So it's easier to pick on the Jews, you see? So they need a scapegoat, and this is not right. We are all people, whether we are fortunate to live a good life, we have always to share. So people have to have a happy place in this world, not to feel that somebody owes him something. Those bigots are the worst apples. They are rotten apples in society. What would you say to those who say the Holocaust never happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are the bigots. That's what I'm talking about, because they want it to happen again. They want to make place for it to happen again. Those are the guys, because there are no bad people. There are bad leaders. If people have good leaders, they are good. Looking at America, I've never run into bad people. Really. Sincerely. I went to-- I want to do something in the house, and I was sent to some people in Parker to get some references and see. I called them up. I said, I want to see the job that they did. Come over with pleasure. And you should see that they invited us, how beautiful, like we would have been old acquaintances. It's that warmth that you can give to people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see the Bible says, 'Love thy neighbor.' How come they don't keep it? This is the most beautiful thing in the Bible, better than anything that I know. 'Love thy neighbor.' And this is what you still have to learn from each other. In closing, is there anything else that you wanted to say or anything that I've forgotten to ask you about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we covered, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I thank you so very much for participating.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my pleasure. I hope that our conversation will have some bearing on future generations for the good of the future generations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
